questions.question: Amongst the properties [reflexivity, symmetry, anti-symmetry, transitivity] the relation R = {(x, y) in N x N | x * y <= x^2} satisfies _________
questions.answer: R satisfies reflexivity, anti-symmetry, and transitivity (i.e., R is a partial order on N)
questions.to_find: Which properties (reflexivity, symmetry, anti-symmetry, transitivity) the relation R satisfies

understand.plain: The relation R on natural numbers pairs (x, y) where x*y <= x^2, which simplifies to y <= x. So (x, y) is in R exactly when y is less than or equal to x. The relation y <= x on N is the standard 'greater-than-or-equal' order, which is reflexive (x <= x), antisymmetric (x <= y and y <= x implies x = y), and transitive (x <= y and y <= z implies x <= z), but NOT symmetric since x < y would not also give y <= x.

keywords.explain: A relation R on A is reflexive if every element relates to itself: for all x, (x, x) in R.
keywords.example: Here x*x = x^2 <= x^2, so (x, x) in R for all x in N — reflexive.

keywords.explain: A relation is antisymmetric if (x, y) in R and (y, x) in R implies x = y.
keywords.example: y <= x and x <= y forces x = y — antisymmetric.

keywords.explain: A relation is transitive if (x, y) in R and (y, z) in R implies (x, z) in R.
keywords.example: z <= y and y <= x implies z <= x — transitive.

understand.visual_alt: The relation R pairs (x, y) where x*y <= x^2, which reduces to y <= x, the standard greater-or-equal partial order on N.

given.aim: Determine which of reflexivity, symmetry, antisymmetry, and transitivity hold for R = {(x, y) in N x N | x*y <= x^2}.

terms.meaning: Relation on N x N defined by x*y <= x^2
terms.connects: Simplifies to y <= x, i.e., the 'greater-or-equal' order

terms.meaning: For x != 0, divide both sides by x to get y <= x
terms.connects: Reduces R to the standard >= order viewed from left element

terms.meaning: A relation that is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive
terms.example: The <= relation on integers is a partial order
terms.connects: R has all three properties so it is a partial order

terms.meaning: R is NOT symmetric since (3, 1) in R but (1, 3) not in R
terms.connects: Symmetry fails whenever x > y

given.plan: Rewrite the condition x*y <= x^2 as y <= x (for x > 0), then test each property by plugging in values and using the definition.

steps.title: Simplify the condition
steps.formula_raw: x*y <= x^2  =>  y <= x  (for x > 0 in N)
steps.apply: Dividing both sides of x*y <= x^2 by x (positive), we get y <= x. So R = {(x, y) | y <= x} on N (excluding 0 if N starts at 1).
steps.note: This is just the >= relation viewed from the left: (x, y) in R iff x >= y.

steps.title: Check reflexivity
steps.formula_raw: Reflexive: for all x, (x, x) in R
steps.apply: x <= x is always true, so (x, x) in R for all x. R is reflexive.

steps.title: Check symmetry and antisymmetry
steps.formula_raw: Symmetric: (x,y) in R => (y,x) in R; Antisymmetric: (x,y) in R and (y,x) in R => x = y
steps.apply: Take x = 3, y = 1: y <= x so (3,1) in R. But x <= y? 3 <= 1 is false, so (1, 3) not in R. Symmetry fails. For antisymmetry: if y <= x and x <= y then x = y — antisymmetry holds.

steps.title: Check transitivity
steps.formula_raw: Transitive: (x,y) in R and (y,z) in R => (x,z) in R
steps.apply: If z <= y and y <= x, then z <= x by transitivity of <=. So (x, z) in R. R is transitive.
steps.note: R satisfies reflexivity, antisymmetry, and transitivity — it is a partial order.

solution.result: R satisfies reflexivity, anti-symmetry, and transitivity (R is a partial order on N). It does NOT satisfy symmetry.

questions.formula_note: The partial-order axioms (reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive) are the key here; simplifying x*y <= x^2 to y <= x makes each check straightforward.

questions.question:
Let R be a symmetric and transitive relation on a set A. Then
A. R is reflexive and hence an equivalence relation
B. R is reflexive and hence a partial order
C. R is reflexive and hence not an equivalence relation
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: The correct statement about a symmetric and transitive relation

understand.plain: A common trap is to think that symmetry + transitivity implies reflexivity. The argument 'since (a,b) in R and R is symmetric, (b,a) in R, so by transitivity (a,a) in R' is flawed because it assumes there exists b with (a,b) in R. An element a that is not related to anything would not satisfy reflexivity. For example, R = {} on A = {1} is symmetric and transitive but not reflexive.

keywords.explain: A relation that is simultaneously reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
keywords.example: Equality = on any set is the simplest equivalence relation.

keywords.explain: Symmetric + transitive does NOT force reflexivity; elements unrelated to everything escape the reflexivity argument.
keywords.example: R = {(1,1)} on A = {1,2} is symmetric and transitive but not reflexive since (2,2) is missing.

keywords.explain: A relation that is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive.
keywords.example: A symmetric partial order would require (a,b) and (b,a) implies a=b, so it reduces to equality.

understand.visual_alt: Consider a set A with an isolated element that has no pair in R; symmetric and transitive conditions are vacuously satisfied for it but reflexivity fails.

given.aim: Determine what additional properties follow from a relation being symmetric and transitive.

terms.meaning: A relation on set A that is symmetric and transitive
terms.example: R = {(1,1)} on A = {1, 2} is symmetric and transitive but not reflexive
terms.connects: Missing reflexivity prevents R from being an equivalence relation

terms.meaning: For all a, b: (a,b) in R implies (b,a) in R
terms.example: (1,2) in R forces (2,1) in R
terms.connects: Together with transitivity it seems to imply reflexivity but does not

terms.meaning: For all a, b, c: (a,b) in R and (b,c) in R implies (a,c) in R
terms.example: If (1,2) and (2,3) are in R then (1,3) must be too
terms.connects: Combined with symmetry the faulty reflexivity argument assumes (a,b) exists for every a

terms.meaning: An element in A with no related pair breaks reflexivity without breaking symmetry or transitivity
terms.example: A = {1, 2}, R = {(1,1)}: (2,2) absent, R still symmetric and transitive
terms.connects: This shows D is the correct choice

given.plan: Find a counterexample where R is symmetric and transitive but not reflexive, eliminating options A, B, C.

steps.title: Identify the faulty argument
steps.formula_raw: Reflexive: forall a in A, (a,a) in R
steps.apply: The 'proof' that symmetric + transitive implies reflexive goes: if (a,b) in R then (b,a) in R (symmetry), so (a,a) in R (transitivity). But this requires an element b with (a,b) in R — an element with NO related pairs is not covered.
steps.note: The argument is only valid for elements that DO appear in some pair in R.

steps.title: Construct a counterexample
steps.apply: Symmetric: (1,1) has its reverse (1,1) in R. Transitive: (1,1)+(1,1) gives (1,1) in R. But (2,2) is not in R, so R is not reflexive. Hence symmetric + transitive does not imply reflexive.
steps.note: Since R is not reflexive, it is neither an equivalence relation nor a partial order — eliminating A, B, C.

solution.result: D. None of the above — a symmetric and transitive relation need not be reflexive.

questions.formula_note: The equivalence relation definition (reflexive + symmetric + transitive) is key; the classic trap is falsely concluding symmetric + transitive implies reflexive, which fails for isolated elements.

questions.question:
Let R be a non-empty relation on a collection of sets defined by A R B if and only if A ∩ B = ∅. Then, (pick the true statement)
A. R is reflexive and transitive
B. R is symmetric and not transitive
C. R is an equivalence relation
D. R is not reflexive and not symmetric
questions.answer: B. R is symmetric and not transitive
questions.to_find: Which properties the disjointness relation on sets satisfies

understand.plain: The relation 'A R B iff A and B are disjoint (share no elements)' is symmetric because disjointness is mutual: if A and B have nothing in common, then B and A have nothing in common. However, it is NOT transitive: A and B can be disjoint, and B and C can be disjoint, but A and C might overlap. For example, A = {1}, B = {2}, C = {1, 3}: A ∩ B = ∅, B ∩ C = ∅, but A ∩ C = {1} ≠ ∅.

keywords.explain: Two sets are disjoint if their intersection is the empty set.
keywords.example: {1, 2} and {3, 4} are disjoint; {1, 2} and {2, 3} are not.

keywords.explain: If A ∩ B = ∅ then B ∩ A = ∅ since intersection is commutative.
A R B and B R C does not imply A R C because B being empty of shared elements with both A and C does not prevent A and C from sharing elements.
keywords.example: A={1}, B={2}, C={1,3}: A R B and B R C hold but A ∩ C = {1} ≠ ∅.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 380 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='380' height='160' rx='10' class='bg'/><circle cx='70' cy='80' r='40' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='70' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><text x='70' y='94' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>{1}</text><circle cx='190' cy='80' r='40' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='190' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><text x='190' y='94' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>{2}</text><circle cx='310' cy='80' r='40' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='310' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C</text><text x='310' y='94' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>{1,3}</text><text x='130' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>A R B</text><text x='250' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>B R C</text><text x='190' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>A ∩ C = {1} ≠ ∅  (not A R C)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three sets A={1}, B={2}, C={1,3}: A and B are disjoint, B and C are disjoint, but A and C overlap at {1}, showing transitivity fails.

given.aim: Determine which properties (reflexive, symmetric, transitive) the disjointness relation A R B iff A ∩ B = ∅ satisfies.

terms.meaning: Relation on a collection of sets: A R B iff A ∩ B = ∅
terms.example: {1,2} R {3,4} is true; {1,2} R {2,3} is false
terms.connects: Also called the disjointness relation

terms.meaning: A R A iff A ∩ A = ∅ iff A = ∅
terms.example: For a non-empty set A, A ∩ A = A ≠ ∅, so A R A fails
terms.connects: Since R is non-empty (contains at least one non-empty set), R is not reflexive

terms.meaning: A ∩ B = ∅ iff B ∩ A = ∅ by commutativity of intersection
terms.connects: R is symmetric

terms.meaning: A={1}, B={2}, C={1,3}: A R B and B R C but not A R C
terms.connects: R is not transitive

given.plan: Check each property: reflexivity (fails for non-empty sets), symmetry (holds by commutativity of intersection), transitivity (fails by counterexample).

steps.title: Check reflexivity
steps.formula_raw: A R A iff A ∩ A = ∅ iff A = ∅
steps.apply: For any non-empty set A in the collection, A ∩ A = A ≠ ∅, so (A, A) is not in R. R is NOT reflexive.
steps.note: The problem states R is non-empty (the collection has at least one non-empty set).

steps.title: Check symmetry
steps.formula_raw: A ∩ B = B ∩ A (commutativity of intersection)
steps.apply: If A ∩ B = ∅ then B ∩ A = ∅, so A R B implies B R A. R is symmetric.

steps.title: Check transitivity with counterexample
steps.formula_raw: A R B and B R C does not imply A R C
steps.apply: Let A = {1}, B = {2}, C = {1, 3}. A ∩ B = ∅ so A R B. B ∩ C = ∅ so B R C. But A ∩ C = {1} ≠ ∅, so A R C fails. R is NOT transitive.
steps.note: This eliminates options A and C; combined with step 2 we confirm B.

solution.result: B. R is symmetric and not transitive.

questions.formula_note: No special formula is needed; the key insight is that symmetry of the disjointness relation follows from commutativity of set intersection, while transitivity fails because B acts as a 'buffer' that can be disjoint from both A and C even when A and C overlap.

questions.question:
Let F be the collection of all functions f : {1, 2, 3} -> {1, 2, 3}. If g, f ∈ F, define an equivalence relation ~ by f ~ g if and only if f(3) = g(3).
a. Find the number of equivalence classes defined by ~.
b. Find the number of elements in each equivalence class.
questions.answer: a. There are 3 equivalence classes (one for each value in {1, 2, 3}). b. Each equivalence class has 9 elements.
questions.to_find: Number of equivalence classes and size of each class for the relation f ~ g iff f(3) = g(3)

understand.plain: Two functions are equivalent if they agree at the single point 3. So the equivalence class of f is determined entirely by f(3), which can be 1, 2, or 3 — giving three classes. Within each class, f(3) is fixed to a particular value (say k), but f(1) and f(2) can each independently be any of {1, 2, 3}. So each class has 3 * 3 = 9 functions.

keywords.explain: The equivalence class [f] is the set of all g with g(3) = f(3).
keywords.example: [f] where f(3) = 2 contains all functions that map 3 to 2.

keywords.explain: The relation partitions F into blocks based on the value at 3; there is one block per possible output value.
Once f(3) is fixed, f(1) and f(2) are free — each has 3 choices independently.
keywords.example: 3 choices for f(1) times 3 choices for f(2) gives 9 functions per class.

understand.visual_alt: The 27 functions from {1,2,3} to {1,2,3} split into 3 groups of 9 based on the value of f(3).

given.aim: Count equivalence classes and their sizes for the relation f ~ g iff f(3) = g(3) on F = {all functions {1,2,3} -> {1,2,3}}.

terms.meaning: Set of all functions from {1,2,3} to {1,2,3}; |F| = 3^3 = 27
terms.example: f where f(1)=1, f(2)=2, f(3)=1 is one of the 27 functions
terms.connects: We partition F using the value at 3

terms.meaning: f and g are equivalent when they agree on the input 3
terms.example: f(3)=1 and g(3)=1 means f ~ g regardless of f(1), g(1), f(2), g(2)
terms.connects: Determines the equivalence class

terms.meaning: Each distinct value of f(3) defines one class; codomain has 3 elements
terms.connects: There are 3 equivalence classes

terms.meaning: f(3) is fixed; f(1) and f(2) each have 3 free choices
terms.example: 3 * 3 = 9 functions per class
terms.connects: Total check: 3 classes * 9 elements = 27 = |F| — correct

given.plan: Identify that [f] depends only on f(3), count distinct values of f(3) for the number of classes, and count free choices at f(1) and f(2) for the class size.

steps.title: Identify the equivalence classes
steps.formula_raw: f ~ g iff f(3) = g(3); equivalence class [f] = {g in F : g(3) = f(3)}
steps.apply: The value f(3) can be 1, 2, or 3. Each distinct value determines one equivalence class: C_1 = {f : f(3)=1}, C_2 = {f : f(3)=2}, C_3 = {f : f(3)=3}. Number of equivalence classes = 3.
steps.note: The number of classes equals the size of the codomain restricted to the image of input 3.

steps.title: Count elements in each class
steps.formula_raw: Functions from an m-element set to an n-element set: n^m
steps.apply: Within C_k (where f(3) = k is fixed), the values f(1) and f(2) are free. Each can independently be any of {1, 2, 3}. So |C_k| = 3 * 3 = 9 functions.

solution.result: a. 3 equivalence classes. b. Each class has 9 elements.

questions.formula_note: The total-functions formula (n^m) helps count class sizes; the key structural insight is that the equivalence class is determined solely by the value at the fixed input point 3.

questions.question:
Let R be a reflexive and transitive relation on a set A. Define a new relation E on A as: E = {(a, b) | (a, b) in R and (b, a) in R}.
a. Prove that E is an equivalence relation on A.
b. Define a relation <= on the equivalence classes of E as [a] <= [b] if (a, b) in R. Prove that <= is a partial order on the equivalence classes.
questions.answer: E is an equivalence relation (reflexive by R's reflexivity; symmetric and transitive by construction). The relation <= on equivalence classes is a well-defined partial order (reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive).
questions.to_find: Proof that E is an equivalence relation and that the induced <= on equivalence classes is a partial order

understand.plain: Starting from a preorder (reflexive + transitive relation) R, we carve out the 'symmetric core' E by keeping only pairs where R goes both ways. This automatically makes E symmetric. Since R is reflexive and transitive, E inherits reflexivity and transitivity. The resulting quotient structure — equivalence classes under E — then inherits a natural well-defined order from R, and this order turns out to be a partial order because antisymmetry is now guaranteed by the construction of the equivalence classes.

keywords.explain: A relation that is reflexive and transitive but not necessarily antisymmetric or symmetric.
keywords.example: The relation 'divides' on integers is a preorder (it is also antisymmetric, but a general preorder need not be).

keywords.explain: E is the symmetric core of R: keep a pair only if R holds in both directions.
keywords.example: If R contains (a,b) and (b,a), then (a,b) in E; otherwise not.

keywords.explain: Collapsing the equivalence classes of E gives a partial order — this is the standard way to turn any preorder into a poset.
keywords.example: If R is divisibility on {2,4,6}, the E-classes might merge 2 and 6 (if 2|6 and 6|2 — impossible here, but illustrates the idea).

understand.visual_alt: A preorder R on A: E captures mutual R-pairs as equivalence classes; the quotient is a Hasse diagram of the partial order on those classes.

given.aim: Prove E = {(a,b) | (a,b) in R and (b,a) in R} is an equivalence relation and that <= on E-classes is a partial order.

terms.meaning: A preorder on A: reflexive and transitive
terms.example: Could be a divisibility-like relation or any reflexive transitive relation
terms.connects: Provides the building blocks for E

terms.meaning: Symmetric core of R: (a,b) in E iff both (a,b) and (b,a) are in R
terms.example: If aRb and bRa, then a and b are in the same E-class
terms.connects: E is symmetric by definition; inherits reflexivity and transitivity from R

terms.meaning: The equivalence class of a under E
terms.example: [a] = {b in A | (a,b) in E}
terms.connects: Serves as an element of the quotient set A/E

terms.meaning: Define [a] <= [b] if (a,b) in R; must show this is well-defined and a partial order
terms.example: If [a] = [a'] and [b] = [b'], then (a,b) in R iff (a',b') in R
terms.connects: Well-definedness follows from E's properties and R's transitivity

given.plan: Verify E is reflexive, symmetric, transitive (using R's properties). Then prove <= on A/E is well-defined, reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive.

steps.title: Prove E is an equivalence relation
steps.apply: Reflexive: R is reflexive, so (a,a) in R and (a,a) in R, hence (a,a) in E. Symmetric: if (a,b) in E then (a,b) in R and (b,a) in R; swapping gives (b,a) in R and (a,b) in R, so (b,a) in E. Transitive: if (a,b) in E and (b,c) in E, then aRb, bRa, bRc, cRb. By transitivity of R: aRb and bRc gives aRc; cRb and bRa gives cRa. So (a,c) in E.
steps.note: E is indeed an equivalence relation.

steps.title: Prove <= on A/E is a partial order
steps.apply: Well-defined: if [a]=[a'] and [b]=[b'], then (a,a') in E so aRa', a'Ra; (b,b') in E so bRb', b'Rb. If aRb then a'Ra, aRb, bRb' gives a'Rb' by transitivity of R. So the definition is independent of representative. Reflexive: (a,a) in R so [a] <= [a]. Antisymmetric: [a] <= [b] and [b] <= [a] means aRb and bRa, so (a,b) in E, meaning [a]=[b]. Transitive: [a]<=[b] and [b]<=[c] means aRb and bRc, so aRc by transitivity of R, giving [a]<=[c].
steps.note: This construction (preorder -> equivalence relation -> partial order on quotient) is a standard technique.

solution.result: E is an equivalence relation on A; the induced <= on the equivalence classes A/E is a partial order.

questions.formula_note: The equivalence-relation and partial-order axioms are both central; the key idea is that the symmetric core E of a preorder R gives a canonical partial order on the quotient set A/E.

questions.question:
The number of equivalence relations on the set {1, 2, 3} is
A. 5
B. 6
C. 9
D. 4
questions.to_find: The number of equivalence relations on a 3-element set

understand.plain: Equivalence relations on a set correspond exactly to partitions of that set. Each partition groups elements into non-overlapping, non-empty blocks, and the equivalence relation holds between elements in the same block. For a 3-element set {1,2,3}, the number of partitions is the Bell number B(3) = 5: one partition with all elements together, three partitions with one singleton and one pair, and one partition with all singletons.

keywords.explain: B(n) counts the number of partitions of an n-element set; B(1)=1, B(2)=2, B(3)=5, B(4)=15.
keywords.example: B(3) = 5 counts all ways to partition {1,2,3} into non-empty blocks.

keywords.explain: A partition splits a set into non-empty, pairwise disjoint subsets (blocks) whose union is the whole set.
Each block of the partition is an equivalence class; two elements are related iff they are in the same block.
keywords.example: Partition {{1,2},{3}} gives equivalence pairs: (1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(1,2),(2,1).

understand.visual_alt: All 5 partitions of {1,2,3}: one whole block, three partitions with a pair and a singleton, and the discrete partition with three singletons.

given.aim: Count all equivalence relations on the 3-element set {1, 2, 3} using the bijection with set partitions.

terms.meaning: The base set with 3 elements
terms.connects: Number of equivalence relations = B(3) = 5

terms.meaning: The number of ways to partition a 3-element set into non-empty subsets
terms.connects: Each partition corresponds to exactly one equivalence relation

terms.meaning: Type {3}: 1 partition; Type {2,1}: 3 partitions; Type {1,1,1}: 1 partition

given.plan: List all partitions of {1,2,3} systematically by block structure, count them; each gives one equivalence relation.

steps.title: List all partitions of {1, 2, 3}
steps.formula_raw: Each partition of A <=> one equivalence relation on A
steps.apply:
Partitions of {1,2,3}:
1. {{1,2,3}} — one class (complete relation)
2. {{1,2},{3}} — 1 pairs with 3 alone
3. {{1,3},{2}} — 1 pairs with 2 alone
4. {{2,3},{1}} — 2 pairs with 1 alone
5. {{1},{2},{3}} — discrete (equality only)
Total: 5 partitions.
steps.note: Systematically: 1 partition with 1 block + 3 partitions with 2 blocks + 1 partition with 3 blocks.

steps.title: Verify using Bell number
steps.apply: The Bell number B(3) = 5 confirms there are 5 partitions and hence 5 equivalence relations on a 3-element set.

questions.formula_note: The bijection between equivalence relations and set partitions is the core formula; the Bell number B(3) = 5 directly gives the answer.

questions.question:
Suppose A is a finite set with n elements. The number of elements in the largest equivalence relation of A is
A. n
B. n^2
C. 1
D. n+1
questions.to_find: The number of elements (ordered pairs) in the largest equivalence relation on an n-element set

understand.plain: An equivalence relation on A is a subset of A x A. The largest equivalence relation is the one with the most ordered pairs. The complete relation A x A — which relates every element to every other element (one single equivalence class containing all of A) — is an equivalence relation (reflexive, symmetric, transitive) and has n^2 pairs. No equivalence relation can have more pairs than A x A, so n^2 is the maximum.

keywords.explain: The equivalence relation with the most ordered pairs; corresponds to the coarsest partition (one big block).
The set of all ordered pairs (a,b) with a,b in A; has n^2 elements when |A|=n.
keywords.example: |A x A| = n * n = n^2.

keywords.explain: The relation A x A is the universal relation: every pair of elements is related.
keywords.example: It is reflexive (all (a,a) included), symmetric (all (a,b) and (b,a) included), transitive (trivially, since every pair is included).

understand.visual_alt: The n x n grid A x A represents all ordered pairs; the universal relation fills every cell with a 1, giving n^2 pairs and one equivalence class containing all elements.

given.aim: Find the maximum number of ordered pairs an equivalence relation on an n-element set A can contain.

terms.meaning: A finite set with n elements
terms.connects: Equivalence relations are subsets of A x A

terms.meaning: Cartesian product: set of all ordered pairs from A; |A x A| = n^2
terms.example: For n=3: A x A has 9 pairs
terms.connects: The largest possible equivalence relation

terms.meaning: R = A x A relates every element to every other element; corresponds to the single-block partition
terms.example: All n^2 pairs are in R
terms.connects: It is reflexive, symmetric, transitive — verified trivially

terms.meaning: The universal relation corresponds to the partition with one block {1,2,...,n}
terms.example: One equivalence class: [1] = [2] = ... = [n] = A
terms.connects: Coarsest partition gives the largest equivalence relation

given.plan: Recognize that the largest equivalence relation is the universal relation A x A; count its pairs as n * n = n^2.

steps.title: Identify the largest equivalence relation
steps.formula_raw: Equivalence relation corresponding to one-block partition: A x A
steps.apply: The coarsest partition of A is {A} itself (one block containing all n elements). The corresponding equivalence relation is A x A — every element is related to every element. This has n * n = n^2 ordered pairs.
steps.note: No equivalence relation can have more than n^2 pairs since A x A is the entire space.

steps.title: Verify A x A is an equivalence relation
steps.formula_raw: Reflexive: (a,a) in A x A for all a. Symmetric: (a,b) in A x A => (b,a) in A x A. Transitive: trivially since all pairs are in A x A.
steps.apply: All three conditions hold for A x A — it is indeed an equivalence relation with n^2 elements.
steps.note: The smallest equivalence relation (discreterelation, equality only) has n elements (just the diagonal pairs); the largest has n^2.

questions.formula_note: The bijection between equivalence relations and partitions is key: the coarsest partition (one block) gives the largest equivalence relation, which is the full Cartesian product A x A with n^2 elements.

formulas.name: Bell Number (Number of Partitions of an n-Element Set)
formulas.plain: B(n) = number of partitions of an n-element set = number of equivalence relations on it; B(0)=1, B(1)=1, B(2)=2, B(3)=5, B(4)=15
formulas.whenToUse: Counting equivalence relations on a finite set, or counting set partitions

terms.means: number of partitions (equivalence relations) on an n-element set
Stirling number of the second kind: number of ways to partition n elements into exactly k non-empty subsets

formulas.trap: Do not confuse with the number of binary relations (2^(n^2)) or the number of total orders (n!); B(n) counts only equivalence relations.
